content.assessment: Delaying tracking to age 14+ requires sustained capacity planning across a multi-year phase-in, precisely when the underlying cohort is contracting at an accelerating and structurally embedded rate for at least two more decades. This creates a genuine planning-horizon tension: the reform's design (phased transition to avoid Poland's abrupt reversal shock) depends on stable-enough cohort projections to sequence capacity changes, yet Hungary's cohort trajectory is itself in flux, potentially requiring the comprehensive lower-secondary structure to be sized against a moving demographic target. Rural viability constraints compound this: comprehensive schools serving contracting rural populations (50-70 school-age children in some settlements) face the same statutory minimum class-size mechanics that threaten current tracks, meaning consolidation into fewer, larger comprehensive schools may be a physical necessity of the reform rather than an incidental side effect.
opportunities: Consolidating the currently geographically fragmented multi-track system into unified comprehensive schools could improve viability precisely in low-density regions where maintaining separate 4/6/8-year pipelines is becoming numerically untenable
strengths: Phased multi-cohort implementation is explicitly designed to avoid disruption, which is prudent given that cohort decline itself is a moving target requiring adaptive capacity planning

A single comprehensive lower-secondary structure may be more resilient to statutory minimum class-size constraints than parallel multi-track (4/6/8-year) pipelines, since consolidating tracks into one structure increases the pool available to meet the 26-28-34 thresholds per school
threats: If phase-in is not carefully sequenced against actual (not projected) cohort figures, the reform risks a Hungarian analog to Poland's double-cohort capacity crisis, amplified by an aging and rurally under-resourced teacher workforce unable to flexibly absorb transitional demand shifts
weaknesses: The reform's timeline for delayed tracking must contend with a 20+ year structural cohort decline whose trajectory is not fully known in advance, making capacity sizing for the comprehensive phase inherently uncertain

Rural regions already facing 10-30% child-population contraction and teacher workforce aging (50% age 50+) face compounding institutional strain that a structural redesign does not directly resolve and may temporarily worsen during transition
